exercises.title: Why streaming, not loading
exercises.prompt: Pre-training corpora are terabytes. Explain why the data pipeline must stream (tokenize/chunk/shuffle/batch on the fly) rather than load everything into memory, and what 'the dataloader must keep up with the GPU' means for training economics.
exercises.hint: TBs don't fit in RAM. Stream from disk/object store, process in a sliding window, shuffle with a buffer. If the dataloader stalls, expensive GPUs sit idle → wasted money. Throughput must exceed GPU consumption.
exercises.solution:
**Why stream instead of load:** pre-training data is **terabytes to petabytes** — it doesn't remotely fit in RAM (or even on one node's disk). So the pipeline must **stream**: read shards from disk/object storage, **tokenize → pack into fixed-length chunks → shuffle → batch** on the fly, holding only a small working set in memory at a time. You process the corpus as a flowing pipeline, never materializing it all.

**Shuffling without full load:** since you can't shuffle a TB-scale array in memory, you use a **shuffle buffer** (read into a buffer of, say, 10k–1M sequences, sample randomly from it, refill) and **pre-shuffled shards**. This approximates global shuffling well enough to decorrelate batches (correlated batches hurt SGD, like in Lesson 9's replay).

**'The dataloader must keep up with the GPU':** GPUs are the **expensive, scarce resource** — a training cluster can cost thousands of dollars per hour. If the data pipeline **can't supply batches as fast as the GPUs consume them**, the GPUs **stall (idle)**, and you're paying full price for partial utilization. So the pipeline must be **engineered for throughput**: parallel reading, multiple worker processes, prefetching (load batch N+1 while the GPU trains on batch N), and tokenization done ahead of time or in parallel. The economic rule is blunt: **GPU idle time = money burned**, so the target is to keep the data pipeline's throughput **strictly above** the GPUs' consumption rate, making training **GPU-bound (good)** rather than **data-bound (wasteful)**. This is why profiling the dataloader (Lesson's objective) is a first-class task — a slow pipeline can silently halve the value of a million-dollar cluster.

exercises.title: Data quality filters and sequence packing
exercises.prompt: Real pre-training pipelines apply quality filters (dedup, language ID, content filtering) and pack tokens into fixed-length sequences. Explain why deduplication matters so much, and implement document packing with proper document-boundary handling (so attention doesn't bleed across unrelated documents).
exercises.hint: Dedup: repeated data → memorization, wasted compute, train/test leakage. Packing: concatenate docs to fill context, but insert EOS and use an attention mask / document IDs so tokens of doc A can't attend to doc B.
exercises.solution:
**Why deduplication matters so much:** web corpora contain **massive duplication** (mirrored pages, boilerplate, reposts, near-duplicate templates). Training on duplicates causes:
- **Memorization & regurgitation:** content seen many times gets **memorized verbatim** (privacy/copyright risk, and it degrades generalization).
- **Wasted compute:** you pay to train on the same content repeatedly instead of learning from diverse data — duplicates can be a large fraction of raw web text.
- **Train/test leakage:** duplicates of benchmark/eval data in the training set **inflate eval scores** (you're testing on training data) — a major source of misleading benchmark numbers.
- **Skewed distribution:** over-represented content biases the model.
Dedup (exact hashing + **near-duplicate detection** like MinHash/SimHash) is one of the highest-ROI quality steps; papers show it improves quality *and* cuts compute.
Other filters: **language ID** (keep target languages, in known proportions), **content filtering** (remove spam, NSFW, PII, low-quality 'word salad'), and **heuristic quality** (length, symbol ratios, perplexity filtering with a small model).

**Sequence packing with document boundaries:**
```python
def pack_sequences(token_docs, seq_len, eos_id):
    """token_docs: list of tokenized documents. Returns packed sequences
       plus a doc-id mask so attention won't cross document boundaries."""
    buf, doc_ids, cur_doc = [], [], 0
    out_tokens, out_docids = [], []
    for doc in token_docs:
        doc = doc + [eos_id]                 # mark end of document
        for tok in doc:
            buf.append(tok); doc_ids.append(cur_doc)
            if len(buf) == seq_len:
                out_tokens.append(buf); out_docids.append(doc_ids)
                buf, doc_ids = [], []
        cur_doc += 1
    return out_tokens, out_docids            # use out_docids to build attn mask

# attention mask: position i may attend to j only if same document AND j<=i
# mask[i][j] = (doc_ids[i] == doc_ids[j]) and (j <= i)
```
**Why boundary handling matters:** to avoid wasting tokens on padding, pipelines **concatenate multiple documents** to fill each fixed-length sequence. But if you do nothing, the **causal attention lets tokens of document B attend to document A** in the same packed sequence — the model learns spurious 'cross-document' dependencies and treats unrelated text as context. The fix: **insert an EOS** between documents and use a **document-aware attention mask** (or per-document attention reset / 'intra-document masking') so each token attends only to **earlier tokens within its own document**. This keeps packing efficient (no padding waste) while preserving correct document semantics. Modern training stacks (and FlashAttention variants) support this 'packed sequences with document masks' directly.

exercises.title: Data is the model: curation, mixtures, and the data wall
exercises.prompt: 'The model is a mirror of its data.' Explain how data composition (domain mixtures, quality weighting, curriculum) shapes the final model, why benchmark contamination is a silent disaster, and how the 'data wall' (finite high-quality tokens) is reshaping pre-training (dedup limits, synthetic data, multimodal/multilingual data). Tie to scaling laws.
exercises.hint: Mixture weights (code %, web %, books %) determine capabilities; quality > quantity. Contamination inflates evals and breaks trust. Chinchilla/over-training need huge token counts but high-quality text is finite → repeat data (diminishing returns), synthetic data (collapse risk), new modalities.
exercises.solution:
**Data composition shapes the model.** The pre-training **mixture** — what fraction is web text vs code vs books vs math vs multilingual vs scientific papers — directly determines the model's **capabilities**. More code → better coding/reasoning; more multilingual → better non-English; more math/textbooks → better reasoning. Labs tune these **mixture weights** carefully (and sometimes via small proxy-model experiments). **Quality weighting** (upsample high-quality sources, downsample low-quality web) and **curriculum** (e.g. higher-quality data later in training) measurably improve results. The blunt truth: a frontier model's character is **mostly a data-curation decision**, and 'better data' has repeatedly beaten 'more parameters' at fixed compute. 'Garbage in → fluent garbage out.'

**Benchmark contamination is a silent disaster.** If copies (or near-duplicates) of **evaluation benchmarks** (MMLU, GSM8K, HumanEval, etc.) leak into the **training set**, the model has effectively **seen the test** — eval scores **inflate** without real capability gains, leaderboards become meaningless, and you make wrong ship/architecture decisions based on fake numbers. It's silent because nothing errors — the scores just look great. Defenses: aggressive **dedup against known eval sets**, **canary strings**, held-out **private evals** (Lesson 10's 'the only eval that matters is YOUR eval'), and decontamination audits. This is why dedup (previous exercise) is as much an *evaluation-integrity* issue as a quality one.

**The data wall and how it's reshaping pre-training.** Scaling laws (Phase 7.13) say loss improves with more **parameters AND tokens** — and deployment-optimal 'over-training' (Llama-style, 10-30× tokens/param) demands *enormous* token counts. But the supply of **high-quality human text is finite** and frontier runs are hitting that ceiling. Responses:
- **Repeating data:** train multiple epochs — but returns diminish fast and over-repetition causes memorization/overfitting (a few passes is okay, many is harmful). Dedup *reduces* available unique tokens, sharpening the wall.
- **Synthetic data:** generate training data with existing strong models (distillation, self-generated reasoning, textbook-style data à la 'Phi'). Powerful, but risks **model collapse / distribution narrowing** if you train on too much of your own (or others') model output without grounding — quality control and mixing with real data are essential.
- **New modalities and sources:** **multimodal** (image/audio/video tokens) and **multilingual** data expand the token pool beyond English web text; code and math are upsampled for reasoning.
- **Shift of investment:** from raw pretraining scale toward **data quality/curation, post-training (SFT/RLHF/RL on verifiable rewards), and inference-time compute** — i.e. *how* you train and *what* you train on, not just *how much*.
**Tie to scaling laws:** scaling laws assume a fixed data distribution and effectively unlimited tokens; the data wall breaks the second assumption, so the clean 'just add tokens' regime is ending. The frontier increasingly competes on **data engineering** — sourcing, dedup, filtering, mixture design, decontamination, and synthetic augmentation — which is exactly why this 'boring' pipeline lesson is one of the highest-leverage in the phase: at the frontier, **the data pipeline *is* the product.**
